Ever thought your Git commits look kind of ugly? Or you sit there wondering what to even write? If so, this post introduces git-cz — a tool that frees you from writing commit messages by hand and makes them prettier. Lazy developer's paradise — not even an exaggeration.
Let's start by talking about why Git commit messages matter. I wrote about this before in
, and one section in there is about Commit Types.
The Commit Type basically signals what kind of change this commit is:
feat - new feature or feature change
style - formatting/style changes that don't affect logic (ESLint, missing semicolons...)
refactor - refactoring/optimization, not a bug or new feature
Even knowing this, in real dev work you often forget what each Commit Type means or aren't sure which one fits — so you end up with ugly commit messages like...
That's from my own blog. A bunch of meaningless commits. Why? Because my lazy gene kicked in and I didn't want to think about which Commit Type to use.
To solve this, let me introduce the tool of the day — git-cz.
is a commit manager that unifies your commit format and makes them prettier. For example:
That's me demoing git-cz. Way better than a raw
, with cute emoji included. That commit message — kind of nice, right?

How do you use it? git-cz has multiple install methods (npm or npx). I'll use npm and install it globally — chances are you'll fall in love with it and want it in other projects too, so global save you from installing it per project.
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
That's it — really. Now we can start using git-cz.
into the project folder, and run:
That's because you haven't staged any files (
). So stage your files first, then run
again — now you'll see git-cz's UI.
Use arrow keys to pick your commit type. These are git-cz's defaults (I'll cover customization later). Select one and press Enter — git-cz will then ask you to fill in the commit description.
After typing the description, hit Enter again and it asks:
Provide a longer description of the change
. This is for a more detailed description. If you don't need one, just hit Enter to skip.
. If there aren't any, hit Enter to skip.
. If there's no related issue, Enter to skip. And your commit is done.
Through this select-and-answer flow, you get a nice clean commit message effortlessly. The conventions are basically the same as my
post, so you can keep using the same mental model — but commit faster, with no format slips or typos. Genuine brain-saver.
One reminder — once you switch to git-cz, you need to adjust your command flow slightly:
That's how you use git-cz correctly.
If git-cz's default commit types aren't enough, you can customize them.
Open your project, create a file called
in the project root. Here's the default config from the official docs, with my added Chinese comments (you can ignore those):
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
'A code change that neither fixes a bug or adds a feature'
'Select the type of change that you
'Write a short, imperative mood description of the change:
'Provide a longer description of the change:
'The packages that this commit has affected
Once you've customized it, git-cz uses your descriptions instead of the defaults.
Quick note: I haven't verified this on Windows, but it works on Mac.
Earlier I covered customization via a project-level
. But adding that file to every project gets tedious. So we can put it globally.
Open your Mac, cd to your home directory (like
there with the same content as above.
Now Global picks up your custom config.
